coredata.dc:title: One-pot synthesis of fluorescent nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for portable detection of iron ion
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Korean Physical Society. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Korean Physical Society. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Yao, Qing
Wu, Haoyi
Jin, Yahong
Wang, Chuanlong
Zhang, Ruiting
Lin, Yujia
Wu, Sijian
Hu, Yihua
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQD) were synthesized by direct thermal decomposition of ammonium citrate tribasic. With the increment of torrefaction temperature, the average size of N-GQD was increased from 2.56 to 3.73 nm. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis (XPS) proved the successful doping of nitrogen atoms. Besides, the N-GQDs showed blue fluorescence which was quenched by Fe3+ ions, and the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs decayed exponentially. Accordingly, the same quenching effect was observed on a test paper prepared by soaking paper in N-GQDs dispersion. The quenching mechanism was due to electron transfer between Fe3+ and functional groups on the surface of N-GQDs which could be confirmed by XPS and diameter growth. Therefore, through this simple method, N-GQDs with high blue fluorescence and high production yield (64%) can be prepared, which provided a new strategy for monitoring and collecting Fe3+ in environmental water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Fluorescence
Fe3+ detection
Fluorescence quenching

We would like to thank Analysis and Test Center of Guangdong University of Technology for TEM and AFM analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of synthesizing N-GQDs and their fluorescence quenching response to the presence of Fe3+ ions.
The TEM images of (a) N-GQDs-16, (b), (c) N-GQDs-18, and (d) for N-GQDs-20, the inset is particle size distribution.
(a) XRD pattern for N-GQDs-16, N-GQDs-18 and N-GQDs-20. (b) FT-IR spectra of N-GQDs-16, N-GQDs-18 and N-GQDs-20. (c) XPS spectra of N-GQDs-18 and high-resolution peaks of C1s (d), N1s (e), O1s (f).

(a) UV–vis absorption, PL, and PLE spectra of the N-GQDs-18. Inset: optical images of N-GQDs-18 under the irradiation of visible light and 365 nm UV light. (b) The concentration-dependence emission intensity of N-GQDs-16, N-GQDs-18 and N-GQDs-20.

(a) Fluorescent emission spectra of N-GQDs-18 as the concentration of Fe3+ (0 mM–10 mM) increases (EX = 350 nm), (b) the relationship between F/F0 and the concentration of Fe3+ (EM = 435 nm, EX = 350 nm), inset: the image of N-GQDs with different concentrations for Fe3+ under UV light (EX = 365 nm), (c) emission spectra of N-GQDs-18 in different pH value, inset: the trend of emission intensity changed, (d) relative fluorescent intensities of the N-GQDs-18 in the absence (black column) and presence (red column) of different metal ions (EM = 435 nm, EX = 350 nm). (e) fluorescence emission of N-GQDs filter paper soaked in different concentrations of Fe3+ (EX = 350 nm) and (f) the trend of emission intensity reduction inset show the image of filter paper under UV light (EX = 365 nm). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Change trend of fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs solution containing Fe3+, (a) is solution and inset is spectra of emission spectrum (b) is filter paper, inset is the spectra of emission spectrum. (c) PL decay time of N-GQDs-18 with 0.0 mM Fe3+, 0.2 mM Fe3+, 0.3 mM Fe3+, 0.4 mM Fe3+. (d) high-resolution peaks of Fe2p for N-GQDs with 0.6 mM Fe3+ and 6 mM Fe3+ (e) FT-IR spectra of N-GQDs-18, N-GQDs-18 + 0.1 mM Fe3+ and N-GQDs-18 + 0.4 mM Fe3+.
Average size distribution of N-GQDs with (a) 0, (b) 0.4 mM Fe3+ and inset is optical image of visible light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right).
Recent progress of N-GQD for Fe3+ detection.

head.ce:title: One-pot synthesis of fluorescent nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for portable detection of iron ion

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Guangdong University of Technology, 510006, HEMC, Guangzhou, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Guangdong University of Technology, 510006, HEMC, Guangzhou, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQD) were synthesized by direct thermal decomposition of ammonium citrate tribasic. With the increment of torrefaction temperature, the average size of N-GQD was increased from 2.56 to 3.73 nm. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis (XPS) proved the successful doping of nitrogen atoms. Besides, the N-GQDs showed blue fluorescence which was quenched by Fe3+ ions, and the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs decayed exponentially. Accordingly, the same quenching effect was observed on a test paper prepared by soaking paper in N-GQDs dispersion. The quenching mechanism was due to electron transfer between Fe3+ and functional groups on the surface of N-GQDs which could be confirmed by XPS and diameter growth. Therefore, through this simple method, N-GQDs with high blue fluorescence and high production yield (64%) can be prepared, which provided a new strategy for monitoring and collecting Fe3+ in environmental water.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as well as their composites, had attracted increasing attention due to the outstanding optical absorbing, fluorescent, or conductive properties available in many potential applications such as bio-labeling [1,2], sensing [3–6], energy storages [7], water treatments [8–10] and so forth. The formation of the CQDs can be determined by the precursor and the processing procedure. Among these, graphene or graphene oxide quantum dots (GQDs) were given enormous interest since they have only a few layers of graphite lattice and oxygen-containing groups (OCGs). Then they were rendered sp2 carbon for the excellent fluorescence and polarization for soluble in water, respectively. Moreover, depending on the synthesis method, their fluorescence was also sensitive to some metal ions, such as Cr(Ⅳ) [11], Au(Ⅲ) [12], Pb(Ⅲ) [13], Fe(Ⅲ) [14], Al(Ⅲ) [15], Ag(Ⅰ) [16], Hg(Ⅱ) [17], Cu(Ⅱ) [18], leading to the feasibility to probe metal concentration in water in a conveniently effectively way, thus monitoring the heavy metal pollution from wastewater in future.

Although GQDs had many expectative advantages, their industrial manufacture and application were still restricted by production yield and luminescence efficiency. In recent years, many types of research have focused on overcoming these challenges, including the improvement of synthesizing conditions for raising the production yield, and adjusting their chemical structure for enhancing the luminescence efficiency [19–21]. Moreover, revealing the luminescence mechanism was another concerning issue for the purpose of better designing the material. For instance, a bottom-up method involving citric acid polymerization and carbonization at 200 °C was employed to synthesize GQDs and the obtained production showed a blue emission at 440 nm [22]. The luminescence efficiency could be enhanced by improving the transition of electrons between the lowest unoccupied molecular orbits (LUMO) and highest occupied molecular orbits (HOMO) by doping. More details for the mechanism of the fluorescence induced by the molecular structure that associates with the LUMO and HOMO, and the phenomena relative to the fluorescence such as intensity enhancement, quenching were expected to be further studied.

Here, a highly producing-yield N doped GQDs (N-GQDs) were synthesized in one-pot torrefaction of an ammonium citrate tribasic mono precursor. Due to the possible unbounded electron of N in graphene lattices, the fluorescence efficiency, as well as the quantum yield, was enhanced as compared to the GQDs derived from citric acid. The fluorescence of the obtained N-GQDs was quenched by the Fe3+ circumstance, showing exponential decay versus the Fe3+ concentration. Such a quenching effect could be also realized on the N-GQDs decorated papers, which were possibly used in portable detection or convenient sampling. Moreover, the mechanism for the fluorescence quenching was found to be the aggregation of N-GQDs, resulting from the bridging N sites and OCGs via Fe3+ ions which had charge transfer to N-GQDs and led to the bigger size of their suspension. The N-GQDs as well as their decorated paper could be potentially used in future metal detection in polluted water.

All chemicals were used of analytical grade and without further purification. The C6H8O7•H2O, C4H6CuO4•H2O, (CH3COO)2Zn•2H2O, K2CO3, were obtained from the Tianjin Damao Chemical reagent factory. The Co(NO3)2•6H2O, NaCO3, C6H8O7•H2O, were obtained from Zhiyuan Chemical reagent Co. Ltd (Tianjin China). The BaCl2•H2O, Cr(NO3)3•9H2O, were obtained from Kermel reagent Co. Ltd (Tianjin China), and the FeCl3•6H2O, PbBr2, were from Macklin. Deionized (DI) water was used to prepare all aqueous solutions.

The fluorescent emission and excitation spectra of the N-GQDs were measured by a Hitachi F-7000 spectrometer. The microstructure and crystalline quality of the as-prepared N-GQDs were observed by a high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM) from FEI Talos F200S. The morphology and size distribution were measured by atomic force microscopes (AFM) form Dimension FastScan. Surface functional groups and chemical composition of the N-GQDs were determined by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) from Thermofisher Nicolet IS50 and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) from Thermo Fisher Escalab 250Xi was employed to study the elements of the samples. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) was employed to study the crystallization degree of N-GQDs on a D8 ADVANCE diffractometer. The Raman spectroscopy was tested by a HORIBA Jobin Yvon LabRAM HR Evolution system. Particle diameter test and Zeta potential for N-GQDs were carried out on a Malvern Instruments, Zetasizer NANO ZS analyzer. The fluorescent lifetime was measured by a FLS-980 fluorescence spectrometer. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were tested by a SHIMADZU, UV-3600 plus spectrophotometer.

The N-GQDs were synthesized in a simple torrefaction, in which ammonium citrate tribasic was directly thermal decomposition and carbonization. Typically, 1.0 g ammonium citrate tribasic was weighed, put into a corundum crucible and kept at a certain temperature for 4 h in an oven. After cooling down to room temperature, the black network-like N-GQD production was obtained and ground into powder (Fig. 1 ). A certain amount of the production was dispersed into DI water under sonication to obtain N-GQDs suspension for the test. The samples prepared at 160, 180 and 200 °C were denoted as N-GQDs-16, N-GQDs-18, and N-GQDs-20, respectively.

The change of fluorescent intensity was employed to test the Fe3+ ions concentration in water due to the quenching effect. At first, 1.0 mg N-GQDs powder was added in 10.0 mL DI water and kept in sonication for 5 min, then 0.1 mg/mL N-GQDs suspension was obtained. The Fe3+, Cu2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Na+, K+, Ba2+, Cr3+, Pb2+ and Fe2+ solution with 100 mM were injected into the suspension step by step. Fluorescent intensity measurement was carried out with 350 nm excitation after their commixing and stabilization homogeneously, and the intensity at 435 nm was monitored. The blank control group was denoted as F0 and another fluorescent intensity with the metal ions group was denoted as F. Besides, in order to measure the sensitivity of N-GQDs to Fe3+, we added 1.0 mL Fe3+ solution with different concentrations (0.1 mM–10 mM) into N-GQDs samples to measure the emission intensity.

In order to provide a convenient and quantitative detection strategy for Fe3+ in polluted water by N-GQDs, we designed a kind of Fe3+ test paper based on N-GQDs decorated filter paper. At first, the filter paper was soaked in the N-GQDs and dried at 90 °C for 1 h in the oven. Then the Fe3+ test paper with fluorescence was obtained. The fluorescence was quenched with immersing in different concentrations of Fe3+ for 1 h. Then the color and fluorescent intensity of the paper could be compared under natural or UV irradiation.

Fig. 1 illustrates a schematically one-pot torrefaction procedure for the N-GQDs. The carbonization and polymerization of ammonium citrate tribasic rendered the formation of GQDs. The decomposition of ammonia and citrate caused the generation of = N–C- roots, which were easily connected with –C=C- bonding and thus form the aromatic N-doped carbon structure. The polymerization of aromatic carbon constructed graphene-like layers, causing the formation N-GQDs. It was noted that the product yield was increased with the increasing amount of raw material. For example, when 2.0 g of the raw material was employed for the torrefaction, about 64% product yield was obtained (Fig. S1), whereas only about is 24.7% product yield was preserved when raw material was changed to citric acid. The morphology of the samples was shown in Fig. 2 . With the increment of torrefaction temperature, the diameter of the N-GQDs grew large. It was increased from 2.56 to 2.99 nm for 160 and 180 °C respectively and further increased to 3.73 nm when the temperature increased to 200 °C. This increment indicated the expansion of the border of the particles due to the number of the carbon atoms that constructed the graphene layer being increased. As shown in Fig. 2 (c), the lattice spacing was revealed as 0.25 nm, which was assigned as the (1120) crystal plane spacing of the lattices [23]. What's more, the AFM characterization further confirmed the uniform spherical shape of NGQDs-18 in Fig. S2.

The XRD patterns of N-GQDs were shown in Fig. 3 (a). They exhibited the broad diffraction peaks centering at 16 and 27°, corresponding to the (001) and (002) planes’ diffraction [24,25], respectively. With the increment of the torrefaction temperature, the peak responsible for the (002) plane was reduced, indicating the number of graphene layers along the [00X] direction was reduced. Then the sp2 carbon was strengthened with the increment of torrefaction temperature. This result could be confirmed by Raman spectra of the samples where the G band resulting from the sp2 vibration was increased (Fig. S3) [26]. Moreover, the FT-IR shown in Fig. 3 (b) indicated that the C=C stretching, C=O stretching and C–N bending vibrational modes were weakened with the increment of the torrefaction temperature. The result was caused by the growth of the N-GQDs whose border was expanded and thus more bonds associated with the carbon and nitrogen were stabilized inside the graphitic structure.

In order to confirm the structure of the N-GQDs, the XPS of the samples was measured and the results were shown in Fig. 3 (c). The 1s peak of carbon shown in Fig. 3 (d) was deconvoluted into four parts, including the peaks at 285.5, 286.3, 287.5, and 288.1eV. They were assigned to the photon from sp2, sp3, C–C/C=C, C–N, and C–O/O=C–O binds [27]. The strong C–N peak indicated the appearance of N as a dopant in the GQDs. The asymmetry band in Fig. 3 (e) was deconvoluted into 3 peaks at 400.1, 401.6, and 402.9 eV, corresponding to the pyrrolic, graphitic, and oxide N in the material [28]. Therefore, the formation of N doping the GQD was probably realized by the substitution to C at the pyrrolic rings at edge with the N–OH groups. The O1s energy shown in Fig. 3 (f) had two obvious peaks at 531.5 and 533.1 eV, corresponding to the C=O and C–OH/C–O–C bonds, respectively [29].

Since the size of the particles was reduced to nano-scale, the quantum confinement rendered the N-GQDs a fluorescence due to the electron transition between LUMO and HOMO. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), a broad PL band was observed with a peak at 435 nm, in agreement with the GQD derived from citrate acid. The PL band could be excited with an excitation around 350 nm, which was demonstrated at PLE and confirmed with excitation-wavelength-dependence PL spectra (Fig. S4). The PL intensity was affected by the concentration of N-GQDs in the solution. As demonstrated in Fig. 4 (b), the PL intensity increased with the increment of N-GQDs concentration, and then reached a peak at 0.09–0.1 mg/mL. The PL intensity was increased when the torrefaction temperature increased from 160 to 180 °C, and then showed a small decrement at 200 °C. Thus N-GQDs-18 at 0.1 mg/mL was the optimized sample according to the PL intensity and it was representative one in some following studies. Moreover, the fluorescent quantum yield of N-GQD was around 14.4%, which was about 24 folds stronger than that of those GQDs derived from citrate acid (0.6%). Therefore, such a one-pot strategy with mono-precursor of ammonium citrate tribasic was facile to obtain a high luminescent GQDs.

In addition, the absorption spectra of the samples were measured and the result in Fig. 4 (a) indicated two obvious peaks at 250 and 350 nm, which were respectively ascribed to the π-π* and n-π* transitions of the N-GQDs. The former was induced by the conjugated sp2 from the aromatic C=C or C=O relative structure [30] while the latter was more probably induced by the OCGs at the edge of the graphene layers. Comparing the absorption and PLE spectra, the n-π* transition appeared more effective than the π-π* transition to excite the fluorescence, implying that the vibration of OCGs at the edge of the N-GQDs contributed to the fluorescence better than the aromatic or graphitic structures. Thus, this fluorescence was sensitive to the surroundings of the surface of the particles.

In order to investigate the surface property of the N-GQDs to the surrounding, metal ions were employed to check their sensitivity. For instance, iron, the important metal in industry, usually appeared in metallurgy and its ionic salts are diffused to the ecosystem through industrial sewage. The detection of iron ions (Fe3+) was advantageous to monitor the diffusion of Fe3+ so as to prevent the environmental pollution caused by it. As compared to mass spectrometry, fluorescence probing was a portable and easy-operation method for metal ion detection. Here, N-GQDs were used to probe the concentration of Fe3+ in water by measuring their PL intensity since they were sensitive to the surrounding solution. As shown in Fig. 5 (a), the PL intensity of N-GQDs-18 was progressively reduced when the titrated Fe3+ ions kept increasing. After normalization, the reducing tendency from 1.00 to 0.45 appeared to be linear with Fe3+ from 0.0 to 1.0 mM. When it exceeded 1.0 mM, the decreasing tendency became slow, and an exponentially decaying curve was observed along with the increment of Fe3+ concentration (Fig. 5 b). Thus, the Fe3+ ions weakened the PL intensity by some effect affecting the surface of N-GQDs. This effect was feasible to probe the Fe3+ concentration in water using PL of N-GQDs as an indicator. The sensitivity of the probing was estimated using the following equation:
(1) S = (I0 - I) / I0
where S represented the sensitivity, I0 and I were the normalized intensity with 0 and any concentration of Fe3+ ions. Therefore, nearly 0.96 sensitivity was observed with Fe3+ concentration reaching 10.0 mM. Moreover, the limit of detection (LOD) for N-GQDs-18 was estimated to be 0.016 μM by a linear correlation (R2 = 0.98) function relationship of y = −0.64867 x + 0.98924 in Fig. 5 (b) and the recent progress of N-GQD for Fe3+ detection is shown in Table 1 . A similar phenomenon was observed in N-GQDs-16 and N-GQDs-20. The Fe3+ could quench the PL of them as a function of exponential decaying, with a near-linear reduction tendency at low Fe3+ concentration. The sensitivity showed in Fig. S5 are 0.94 and 0.92 for N-GQDs-16 and N-GQDs-20, respectively.

In addition, since the sensitivity of the N-GQDs edge, the pH value also influenced the edge atom vibration and thus affected the PL intensity. As shown in Fig. 5 (c), with the pH tuning from acid to neutral, the PL peak shifted from 475 (pH∼2) to 455 (pH∼4) and then progressively to 435 nm (pH∼7) with about 1 fold near-linear PL intensity increment. The further increasing pH from 7 to 11 reduced the intensity back to the acidic circumstance without shifting the peak position. It was noted that the PL quenching by Fe3+ was also observed in acidic circumstances. As shown in Fig. S6, an exponentially decaying tendency was also observed in both pH ∼4 and ∼2 cases. However, the sensitivity was reduced to about 0.78 and 0.50 for pH ∼4 and ∼2, respectively, indicating that acidic circumstance was unfavorable for high-sensitivity Fe3+ probing. What's more, the selectivity of N-GQDs to many metal ions was tested additionally, including the Cu2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Na+, K+, Ba2+, Cr3+, Pb2+ and Fe2+. As shown in Fig. 5(d), the PL of N-GQDs-18 for these metal ions showed different sensitivity at 10 mM. As compared to the Fe3+ which caused the sensitivity of 0.94, the Cu2+, Cr3+, and Pb2+ could be achieved around 0.5, while Co2+ was 0.6. The quenching effect induced by other ions was small. Therefore, it indicated that N-GQDs with excellent PL property had promising application in the highly-sensitive and highly-selective detection of Fe3+.

In order to realize the portable and on-the-spot environmental monitoring, the N-GQDs were decorated on cellulose to fabricate a test paper. Fig. 5 (e) demonstrated the PL of the paper. It was observed that the paper with N-GQDs decoration showed a blue PL emission band at 450 nm and gradually decrease with the concentration of Fe3+ increase under UV excitation in Fig. 5 (f). The decorating-time-dependence PL intensity was stabilized in about 60 min decoration time with an error bar of 12.7 (Fig. 6 a). That meant a test paper was fabricated in 60 min. This PL was quenched after immersing in various concentrations of Fe3+ aqueous solution, and an exponentially decaying tendency was observed with the increasing of Fe3+ concentration. The sensitivity of the probing from 0.0 to 7.5 mM was about 0.89. The immersing-time-dependence PL intensity demonstrated that the quenching effect of Fe3+ on N-GQDs took about 60 min to reach an equilibrium (Fig. 6 b). Duration of the immersing of test paper was about 60 min. Besides, we also tested the FT-IR spectrum of blank test paper in Fig. S7. Therefore, the feasibility that using N-GQDs decorated test paper to monitor the industrial Fe3+ emission and diffusion were demonstrated.

The quenching mechanism of the N-GQDs induced by Fe3+ was possibly caused by a charge transfer process and the following aggregation. In a charge transfer process, the edge atoms on the surface of N-GQDs, especially for the pyrrolic-N with an unpaired electron, had an affinity to the positive-charge Fe3+ ions because the ions had a large potential to be stabilized by the ligands [38]. Then the unpaired electron had a tendency to transfer from N-GQDs to Fe3+. The 2p 1/3 and 2p 2/3 energy of Fe2+ observed from the XPS confirmed the charge transfer process (in Fig. 6 (d)). As a result, a dynamic quenching occurred and it was confirmed by the shortening of the fluorescent lifetime (in Fig. 6 c). This dynamic quenching resulted in the initial reduction of PL intensity, especially for those N-GQDs decorated on cellulose papers. For the suspension, with the increase of Fe3+ ions, the amount of the detectable Fe2+ seemed to remain the same (Fig. 6 (d)). Thus, the further reduction of fluorescence intensity was induced by some other reasons.

Previous researches had reported that Fe3+ had a high affinity for N and O on the surface of C-dots [39,40]. Besides, Fe3+ could attract those functional groups distributing on the surface of C-dots including –NH2, –COOH, and –OH [41,42]. As a charge transfer occurred between N-GQDs and Fe3+ ion, chelation or coordination would be formed and thus caused the aggregation of N-GQDs so that surface atoms’ vibration was prevented. As demonstrated by the PLE, the n-π* transition appeared more effective than the π-π* transition to excite the fluorescence, implying that the vibration of OCGs at the edge contributes to the fluorescence better than the aromatic or graphitic structures. As a result, the preventing vibration led to further fluorescence quenching. In order to find out functional groups change on the surface of N-GQD-18 after adding Fe3+ into the solution. We set up three control groups (N-GQDs-18 with 0.0 mM Fe3+, 0.1 mM Fe3+ and 0.4 mM Fe3+) and took the FT-IR test. As shown in Fig. 6 (e), when N-GQDs exist Fe3+, the C–O bond peak was obviously weakened to disappear, this phenomenon may cause by Fe3+ combined with C–O bond and formed chelation or coordination. After 60 min, the average size distribution of N-GQDs had a significant increase caused by visible agglomerates appearing in the solution (N-GQDs-18 with 0.4 mM Fe3+) and fluorescence intensity greatly reduced under the irradiation of UV light as shown in Fig. 7 .

In summary, we have synthesized blue fluorescence quantum dots with a quantum yield of about 14.4% through a simple one-pot thermal decomposition and found that Fe3+ had significant selectivity and sensitivity to the quenching of N-GQDs. The Fe3+ had an affinity to attach to the oxygen-containing groups on the surface of N-GQDs and caused charge transfer to form N-GQDs to Fe3+ linking group, which appeared obvious agglomerates in the solution and causes the reducing vibration of some surface functional groups, such as the C–O bond. Besides, N-GQDs were available to decorate on the test paper to directly detect the concentration of Fe3+ ions with fluorescence quenching. Therefore, such facile-synthesis N-GQDs could be potentially used in industrial wastewater monitoring so as to control their diffusion.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.51972065) and Guangdong Natural Science Foundation (No. 2021A1515010179). We would like to thank Analysis and Test Center of Guangdong University of Technology for TEM and AFM analysis.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Transcriptomic response and perturbation of toxicity pathways in zebrafish larvae after exposure to graphene quantum dots (GQDs)
J. Hazard Mater.
